mnemonics: B.U.D.S. for Discrete Distributions: Binomial (count successes), Uniform (all equal), Discrete (specific values), Special (like Poisson for rare events).
C.U.N.T. for Continuous Distributions: Continuous (infinite values), Uniform (equal over range), Normal (bell curve), T-distribution (small samples).
P.M.F. for Probability: P(robability) M(ass) F(unction) is for discrete, like counting marbles in a bag.
keyPoints: A probability distribution describes how probabilities are distributed over the values of a random variable.
Distributions are either discrete (countable outcomes) or continuous (measured outcomes).
The sum of all probabilities in a discrete distribution must equal 1; the total area under the curve of a continuous distribution must equal 1.
commonMistakes: Confusing a probability distribution with a single probability value (e.g., P(X=2) vs. the entire function P(X=x)).
Using a probability mass function (PMF) for a continuous variable or a probability density function (PDF) for a discrete variable.
Assuming the value of a PDF at a point is a probability; for continuous distributions, only intervals have probability, calculated as the area under the curve.
